query: clinical features of tuberous sclerosis complex including renal angiomyolipomas and seizures

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with tuberous sclerosis are best managed with an interprofessional team approach. Specialties include primary care providers, pediatrics, dermatology, nephrology, neurology, neurosurgery, cardiology, ophthalmology, radiology, surgery, and interventional radiology. Primary care providers and pediatricians should provide annual examinations for patients with tuberous sclerosis, focusing on the common manifestations and complications of tuberous sclerosis, and conduct a thorough multisystemic history and physical. Several clinics in the United States specialize in the follow-up and care of patients with tuberous sclerosis. Referral of patients to these centers should ensure that they will receive a comprehensive follow-up. When this is impossible, individual specialty referral should be conducted for the different manifestations, such as epilepsy, lymphangioleiomyomatosis, and renal angiomyolipomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Cystic Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberous sclerosis: This condition is due to mutations in the genes TSC1 and TSC2, which result in hamartoma formation throughout the body. Patients can present with several characteristic findings, including angiofibroma, facial nevi, cardiac rhabdomyoma, epilepsy, and mental retardation. Renal findings include angiomyolipomas, renal cysts, and renal cell carcinomas. One study showed that about 71% of patients had renal lesions; the most common was angiomyolipomas (61%), and 28% had renal cysts. [15] [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Angiomyolipoma -- Etiology -- Causes of Renal Angiomyolipomas
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberous sclerosis is usually diagnosed by the discovery of benign tumors in the brain, kidneys, heart, liver, and lungs. Initial symptoms often manifest as neurological, including seizures, mental deficiencies, autism, behavioral challenges (such as obsessive-compulsive disorder or overly aggressive tendencies), learning disabilities, and developmental delays. In addition, this condition is also associated with various skin manifestations. A definitive diagnosis can usually be made by genetic testing for the TSC1 or TSC2 genes. [15] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Tuberous Sclerosis ," for further information.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac lesions may be present in 50% to 70% of patients at some time. As noted above, the lesions can cause neonatal death, but they often regress by early childhood. [2] A case series involving 125 patients reported that cardiac rhabdomyomas were the second most common presentation of TSC in early childhood or infancy (second to seizures). [9] Renal angiomyolipomas may manifest in 55% to 75% of patients. [6] [30] Incidence increases with age, and a longitudinal study showed a 75% prevalence of renal angiomyolipomas by 10.5 years. [30] These angiomyolipomas are often asymptomatic, but symptoms can include flank pain, hemorrhage, hematuria, and a tender abdominal mass. [20] [31] The diagnosis of tuberous sclerosis, discussed below, consists of major and minor clinical criteria. [2] The diagnosis is often made during childhood, when several dermatological features may become apparent. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) is a genetic disorder marked by benign tumors, or hamartomas, forming in organs such as the brain, skin, kidneys, heart, lungs, and eyes. These growths cause varied symptoms, including seizures, developmental delays, intellectual disabilities, skin abnormalities, and kidney dysfunction. The condition’s severity differs widely, with some individuals experiencing mild effects and others facing significant challenges. Diagnosis requires a multidisciplinary approach, integrating clinical assessments, imaging, and genetic testing. Although no cure exists, treatment focuses on symptom management to enhance quality of life. TSC’s diverse presentation, with manifestations like cardiac rhabdomyomas appearing prenatally and renal or pulmonary lesions emerging in adulthood, complicates timely diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Angiomyolipoma -- Etiology -- Causes of Renal Angiomyolipomas
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberous sclerosis: This is a rare, familial, autosomal dominant genetic disorder characterized by the development of benign tumors in various body parts, typically affecting the brain, eyes, heart, lungs, and kidneys. Tuberous sclerosis is also associated with adenoma sebaceum, epilepsy, and mental deficiencies. Although the overall incidence of tuberous sclerosis is estimated at about 1 per 10,000 population, it is believed to be significantly underdiagnosed. Tuberous sclerosis does not exhibit racial or sexual predilection. Over 50% of individuals with tuberous sclerosis will eventually develop renal angiomyolipomas. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for TSC includes the following: Each patient will present different manifestations of the disease and will, therefore, follow different clinical courses. [4] The morbidity of TSC is quite significant for patients and caregivers. In addition, mortality is significantly higher than in patients without TSC, and various long-term studies report mortality at 4.8% to 8.3% at a follow-up range of 8 to 17.4 years. [37] Complications of seizures or renal angiomyolipomas most often cause mortality. [37] Other causes of mortality include bronchopneumonia, complications of LAM, and neonatal heart failure related to rhabdomyomas. [14] The 5-year survival rate of patients receiving lung transplants due to LAM is about 65%. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify clinical manifestations indicative of tuberous sclerosis complex across diverse age groups and developmental stages. Determine appropriate imaging modalities for the screening and surveillance of tuberous sclerosis-related lesions in affected individuals. Implement evidence-based management strategies for tuberous sclerosis-related neurological, dermatological, renal, pulmonary, and systemic manifestations. Collaborate with interprofessional specialists from various disciplines to ensure comprehensive care and support for individuals with tuberous sclerosis complex. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: The following healthcare professions can provide consults for this condition: Pediatrics Nephrology Neurology Neurosurgery Cardiology Ophthalmology Dermatology

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Renal angiomyolipomas are present in about 75% of patients by age 10. The likelihood of bleeding or other complications is related to size, vascularity, and other factors. Mortality is highly linked to complications of seizures and renal angiomyolipomas. Lymphangioleiomyomatosis is the presence of smooth muscle tissue in the lungs associated with cystic loss of lung parenchyma; it is most common in women of childbearing age, and while many patients with TSC have radiographic evidence of LAM, symptomatic LAM is much less common. Many experts recommend against estrogen-containing birth control pills, as this could exacerbate LAM progression in women. Progesterone-only pills do not seem to have the same exacerbating effect. Pregnancy must be undertaken cautiously for the same reason, and pregnant women require frequent monitoring. The recognition that mTOR inhibitors (rapamycin, sirolimus, everolimus) greatly slow the progression of hamartomas in many tissues, including the brain, kidney, lung, and skin, has greatly changed the management of TSC. Surgical management is still warranted in some cases, and genetic counseling is advised, given the autosomal dominant nature of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Fahr Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis -- 4. Congenital Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: It is an autosomal dominant neurocutaneous syndrome with a mutation in TSC1 and TSC2 genes. It exhibits features involving the skin (ash-leaf macules, shagreen patch, adenoma sebacum, angiofibroma), kidneys (angiomyolipoma), cardiac (rhabdomyosarcoma) and CNS (subependymal nodules, hamartomas, cortical and subcortical tubers which can get calcified). The frequency of calcifications increases with age. Coats plus syndrome (CRMCC-Cerebroretinal microangiopathy with calcifications and cysts)

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- Pathophysiology -- Affected Areas and Organs
retrieved_docs.content: Renal angiomyolipoma and cysts may affect renal function; complications include pain, bleeding, and possible renal failure. Angiomyolipomas are very common, affecting up to 75% of patients. Please see StatPearls’ accompanying reference, “ Renal Angiomyolipoma ,” for further information. More severe but less common renal manifestations include polycystic kidney disease (PKD) and renal cell carcinoma, with incidences of about 5% and 3%, respectively. [14] The TSC2 and PKD1 genes are adjacent; therefore, a TSC2 mutation can partially or completely accompany a PKD1 mutation. This is called the contiguous gene syndrome. The renal phenotype in these patients is often more severe, with early progression to renal failure. In addition, some patients with TSC have simple cystic disease without a PKD1 mutation, and phenotypically, these patients are often indistinguishable. [14] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberous sclerosis affects multiple systems and may manifest in various tissues within a lifetime. As detailed above, renal manifestations are common in patients with tuberous sclerosis complex, especially renal angiomyolipomas. Other common renal lesions include polycystic kidney disease, renal cysts, and renal cell carcinomas. The latter has a similar lifetime risk to the general population; however, they usually present at a younger age. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) is a genetic disorder inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion and is characterized by an increased predisposition to hamartoma formation. [1] TSC results from mutations in the genes TSC1 and TSC2 and is known for causing neurological disorders, including epilepsy and intellectual disability. [2] Other organ systems involved include pulmonary, renal, dermatologic, and cardiac. TSC is usually diagnosed in childhood or infancy, and the affected individuals may present with developmental delay, skin manifestations, or seizures. However, it may also be diagnosed earlier or later based on various clinical manifestations. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberous Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This educational activity provides clinicians with skills to recognize TSC’s varied clinical presentations and timelines, improving early detection and intervention. Participants learn to interpret diagnostic tools like imaging and genetic tests and develop tailored treatment plans. Collaboration with an interprofessional team, including neurologists, geneticists, nephrologists, and nurses, enhances patient outcomes by ensuring coordinated care. This teamwork facilitates comprehensive management, reduces diagnostic delays, and optimizes symptom control, ultimately improving quality of life for patients and their families across all life stages.

retrieved_docs.title: Clear Cell Renal Carcinoma (Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with tuberous sclerosis present with benign tumors in various organs including, kidneys, liver, lung, brain, heart, eyes, and skin. It is characterized by ash-leaf spots, shagreen patches, hamartomas, and angiomyolipomas, whereas, VHL is characterized by hemangioblastomas and benign and malignant tumors. Bilateral RCCs are more common in patients with papillary RCC, underlying history of VHL disease, and tuberous sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.content: Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the severe renal vascular changes seen in malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis share comparable clinical manifestations, risk factors, and disease mechanisms with complicated primary hypertension and hypertension that arises secondary to other underlying conditions. This similarity underscores the importance of recognizing overlapping signs and symptoms when diagnosing and managing patients with advanced hypertensive organ damage and secondary causes of elevated blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these distinct neonatal and genetic forms of diabetes are considered subtypes of diabetes mellitus. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting a general retinal disorder that can occur in diabetic patients, and more specifically with اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), a hallmark microvascular complication of diabetes. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية (end‑stage renal disease), demonstrating that chronic diabetes can progress to severe kidney failure. The relationship between اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ and مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية further underscores the shared microvascular pathology that links diabetic eye disease and diabetic kidney disease, highlighting the importance of early detection and integrated management of these complications in patients with any form of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension and hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension are both interpreted as manifestations of blood pressure (ضغط الدم) abnormalities. Blood pressure (ضغط الدم) is related to low blood pressure (ضغط الدم المنخفض), highlighting the continuum between elevated and reduced arterial pressure states. Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that this severe renal vascular disease shares many of the same clinical features—such as marked vascular injury and renal impairment—as complicated and secondary forms of hypertension. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians recognize the spectrum of hypertensive disorders and their renal complications, facilitating more accurate diagnosis and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 921
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is identified and interpreted through increased blood pressure measurements, which are central to its clinical assessment. Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis exhibits clinical features that are similar to those of essential hypertension, highlighting overlapping manifestations and the need to consider both conditions when evaluating patients with elevated blood pressure and related renal changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2554
retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis is classified as a subtype of اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية (renal vascular disorder) and shares clinical characteristics with essential hypertension, indicating that patients with this form of arteriolar kidney disease often present with blood‑pressure patterns and target‑organ effects similar to those seen in essential hypertension. Recognizing this relationship helps clinicians differentiate primary hypertensive disease from secondary renal vascular pathology and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2397
retrieved_docs.content: The brain (mozek) is the anatomical location for a range of pathological findings and a key physiological process. Encephalitis is located in the brain. The brain serves as the finding site for a brain cyst (كيسة دماغية), a metabolic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ), and a toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي). In addition, the brain is the site of the biological process of thinking (myšlení). This underscores the brain’s central role in both disease manifestation and cognitive function, highlighting its importance in medical evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis is classified as a subtype of اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية, indicating that it represents a specific form of renal vascular disorder. The Vascular System is identified as the anatomic site associated with both other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease and with اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية, underscoring its central role in the pathophysiology of cerebrovascular and renal vascular conditions. Recognizing these interconnections highlights the importance of evaluating the vascular system across multiple organ systems to inform comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all identified as specific manifestations or related entities of مرض السكري (diabetes). In clinical practice, مرض السكري is linked not only to its primary metabolic disturbances but also to other pancreatic disorders that are distinct from diabetes itself, highlighting the broader spectrum of pancreatic pathology that may coexist with or mimic diabetic presentations. Additionally, مرض السكري is associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder), reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy and other vision‑related complications in patients with diabetes. Furthermore, مرض السكري is recognized as having a child condition, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome), underscoring the interrelationship between diabetes and the cluster of metabolic risk factors that include obesity, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance. Together, these connections emphasize the heterogeneous nature of diabetes, its potential neonatal and developmental presentations, its association with other pancreatic and ocular disorders, and its role as a central component of metabolic syndrome, all of which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1847
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension is interpreted as ضغط الدم (blood pressure), emphasizing that this complex form of high blood pressure is fundamentally a disorder of blood pressure regulation. ضغط الدم is directly related to ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure), indicating that the spectrum of blood pressure abnormalities—from elevated to reduced levels—are interconnected clinical entities. Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, reflecting that severe arteriolar damage in the kidneys can present with features indistinguishable from complicated or secondary forms of hypertension. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of evaluating both high and low blood pressure states and recognizing their potential overlap with serious renal vascular pathology such as malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis for accurate diagnosis and optimal patient management.
